Figure 5
Comparison of Tilted-PIC and Tilted-CBS against CBS across 96 scenarios. (a) Win/Tie/Loss composition
by distribution. (b) Heatmap of Tilted-PIC’s average cost change vs CBS by lead time and penalty ratio;
green indicates improvement. (c) Mean cost reduction by distribution. (d) Optimized proportional gain
Kp as a function of lead time (solid, decreasing) and penalty ratio (dashed, increasing); the horizontal
reference at Kp=1 corresponds to CBS behavior.


--- Page 31 ---
Huang et al : AlphaInventory: Evolving White-Box Inventory Policies with Deployment Guarantees
31
5.3.4. Structural Interpretation
We provide a qualitative interpretation of the above experi-
mental results, without pursuing a formal analysis since it is not the main focus of this paper. For a
rigorous treatment of CBS, we refer to Xin (2021).
A useful perspective is that lost-sales systems with positive lead times involve two structural
frictions: over-aggressive replenishment after demand spikes and slow recovery after severe inventory
depletion. CBS adopts a fixed target level together with a fixed order cap. While this design is robust
and easy to tune, it is inherently rigid. It may react too aggressively when the inventory gap is
moderate, and too conservatively when the gap is large.
The first friction arises because lost demand does not accumulate as backlog. After a temporary
demand spike, CBS attempts to close the full inventory gap via min(∆n,r). When demand returns to
normal, this aggressive response may lead to excess inventory and increased holding cost, especially
under long lead times. Tilted-PIC addresses this issue through the proportional factor Kp < 1, which
dampens the replenishment response and reduces overshooting.
The second friction occurs after large stockout events. When IPn falls far below S, the fixed cap
r limits the recovery speed, creating a bottleneck. This effect is amplified by positive lead times,
which delay replenishment arrivals. Tilted-CBS addresses this issue by introducing an elastic cap
rbase + α∆n, which increases the allowable order size when the gap is large. Tilted-PIC inherits this
mechanism.
Overall, Tilted-CBS improves upon CBS by relaxing the fixed-cap constraint in large-gap states,
while preserving the base-stock structure. Tilted-PIC further enhances performance by combining
proportional damping with an elastic cap, allowing it to respond conservatively when the gap is
small and aggressively when the gap is large.
The gains are most pronounced under high demand variability. In such environments, both over-
reaction and slow recovery occur more frequently. The elastic cap accelerates recovery after large
shocks, while proportional damping reduces over-ordering after temporary spikes. This combination
leads to consistent improvements over CBS in high-variance settings.
6. Conclusion
This paper studies how to evolve executable white-box inventory policies with large language models
under distribution shift. To address this problem, it develops a unified framework that integrates
training, inference, and deployment. Instead of focusing only on gains from historical data, the
framework explicitly incorporates structural validity, replay-based certification, and deployment
safety into the policy-evolution process. The analysis establishes a coherent guarantee chain across
these three stages. During training, the method concentrates proposal mass on structurally valid
policies with high success rates. During inference, it translates proposal quality into period-level


--- Page 32 ---
Huang et al : AlphaInventory: Evolving White-Box Inventory Policies with Deployment Guarantees
32
certified improvement through confidence-screened replay evaluation. During deployment, it remains
conservatively safe while allowing a dynamic gap bound relative to the oracle-safe benchmark. Taken
together, these results show that LLM-based policy evolution is not only effective in weakly structured
inventory environments, but also statistically disciplined and operationally deployable.
The empirical results further demonstrate the practical value of the framework. After training the
AlphaInventory reasoner with GRPO, the model substantially outperforms the base model on both
the synthetic out-of-distribution benchmark and the real-world retail benchmark. It also achieves
stronger task completion and broader cross-domain generalization. In addition, in the classical
lost-sales setting, the framework evolves structurally meaningful extensions of strong benchmark
policies, including Tilted-CBS and Tilted-PIC. This finding suggests that generative policy search
can serve not only as a competitive decision tool, but also as a mechanism for discovering new
inventory-policy structures. Overall, the paper suggests that generative AI may support inventory
management while preserving interpretability and deployment discipline. This perspective points to
a potentially useful direction for data-driven yet auditable policy design in operations management.


--- Page 33 ---
Huang et al : AlphaInventory: Evolving White-Box Inventory Policies with Deployment Guarantees

Online Supplement
Appendix A: Notation Summary
Global indexing conventions. Throughout the paper, we use the following indexing convention
unless stated otherwise. In particular, in Tables 10 and 11, t denotes the outer policy-update epoch.
Table 8
Global indexing conventions
Symbol Meaning
t
outer policy-update / deployment epoch
j
inner AlphaInventory proposal round
r
calendar-day index in synthetic or real datasets
n
period index in the standalone classical inventory model of Section 5.3
To improve readability, we split notation into stage-specific tables.
Table 9
Training-stage notation
Notation
Meaning
K, η
Number of training update steps and training update step size.
eΠ, Π, can(·), g(π)
Raw code space, canonical policy space, canonicalization map,
and structural-validity indicator.
x ∼Dtr, Πbase
Training instance and shared baseline-policy family used in
binary reward evaluation.
r(x,π), ∆(π)
Binary training success indicator and success probability under
Dtr.
Gtr, τgood, ∆bad, γ
Training good region, training-good threshold, bad-region enve-
lope, and separation margin.
pk, b∆k(π), Etr, εK, ρK
Training proxy proposal law, estimated success, concentration
event, cumulative estimation error budget, and bad-to-good mass
ratio bound.

Meaning

policy, and period-specific reference baseline.

|
˜π),

|
˜π), bvt(π | ˜π), radt(π | ˜π)
Replay window, sample-path gain, empirical replay mean/vari-
ance, and pairwise confidence radius.
mt, Bt, Nt, Ct
Replay sample size, bounded-gain constant, period-start evalua-
tion budget, and period-level uniform confidence event.
{Ft,j}J
j=0, Gt(π, ˜π)
Period-t AlphaInventory filtration and the query-time sigma-field
for an evaluated policy pair.
LCBt, UCBt
Lower and upper confidence bounds used for screening and
optimistic selection.
St(π), It,j(π), Ot,j(π)
Empirical safety, improvement, and optimistic screening scores
used by the round-j gate.
∆safe
t
(π), ∆imp
t,j (π)
Replay-population safety and improvement margins used in pro-
motability analysis.
Gprom
t,j
(ε), Eprom
t
(ε)
Round-j promotable set and period-level certified promotion
event.
Qt,j, πt,j, πch
t,j, πch
t,0, At,0, At,j, AtRound-j proposal law, generated candidate, running champion
and initial champion, and initial/evolving/final active pools.
Afeas
t,0 , Afeas
t
, Et
Initial and final safety-feasible active subsets, together with the
period-level evaluated policy–comparator pair set.
τt, κt, ¯κt, qt, ¯qt
Contextual-distortion and overlap terms, together with the
induced effective one-round coverage lower bounds for promotion
and safe search.
Table 11
Deployment-stage notation
Notation
Meaning
dt
Policy finally deployed at period t.
V rep
t
(π | ˜π), V dep
t
(π | ˜π)
Replay gain and deployment gain of policy π relative to com-
parator ˜π.

t
, P dep
t
, ξt
Replay/deployment path distributions and their IPM mismatch
bound.

t
, V safe,∗
t
Certifiably safe policy class over Π and the corresponding oracle
safe value.
Πsafe,νt
t
, νt
Near-oracle safe class and search-approximation tolerance.
Γt(˜πt,dt), Dt, GT
Per-period oracle-safe deployment-gap bound, period-level safe-
class discovery event, and horizon-level rolling good event.
Appendix B: Proofs of the Main Results
This appendix provides proofs fully aligned with the revised interface assumptions in the main text.

B.1. Proof of Theorem 1
For any policy subset S ⊆Π, define
WK(S) ≜
X
π∈S
p0(π)exp
(
η
K
X
k=1
b∆k(π)
)
,
(B. 1)
which represents the total unnormalized weight of all policies in S after K steps of exponential
reweighting. At step K, a straightforward induction argument based on the recursive update in (2)
gives
pK(π) =
p0(π)exp
n
η PK
k=1 b∆k(π)
o
P
π′∈Π p0(π′)exp
n
η PK
k=1 b∆k(π′)
o.
By comparing this expression with the definition in (B. 1), we obtain that, for any subset S ⊆Π,
pK(S) = WK(S)
WK(Π).
(B. 2)
By (B. 2), the comparison of probability masses reduces to the comparison of the corresponding
unnormalized weights. Therefore, in the sequel, it is sufficient to compare WK(·) across different
subsets, rather than tracking the distribution pK directly, since all such quantities share the same
denominator WK(Π).
By assumption, there exists an event Etr such that, for any π with p0(π) > 0,
K
X
k=1
b∆k(π) = K∆(π) +
K
X
k=1
␀b∆k(π) −∆(π)

.
The second term is bounded in absolute value by εK. Hence, we immediately obtain
K∆(π) −εK ≤
K
X
k=1
b∆k(π) ≤K∆(π) + εK.
Next, we apply this bound to the good region Gtr and its complement.
1. For π ∈Gtr, the definition of Gtr implies that ∆(π) ≥τgood. Therefore, PK
k=1 b∆k(π) ≥Kτgood−εK.
Substituting this lower bound into (B. 1) yields
WK(Gtr) ≥p0(Gtr)exp

η(Kτgood −εK)
	
.
2. For π ∈Π \ Gtr, the definition of the complement region gives PK
k=1 b∆k(π) ≤K∆bad + εK. By
the same argument,
WK(Π \ Gtr) ≤p0(Π \ Gtr)exp

η(K∆bad + εK)
	
.

Huang et al : AlphaInventory: Evolving White-Box Inventory Policies with Deployment Guarantees
4
Now, taking the ratio yields
WK(Π \ Gtr)
WK(Gtr)
≤p0(Π \ Gtr)
p0(Gtr)
exp

−η(Kγ −2εK)
	
= ρK.
By (B. 2),
pK(Π \ Gtr)
pK(Gtr)
= WK(Π \ Gtr)
WK(Gtr)
≤ρK,
proving (3). Since pK(Gtr) + pK(Π \ Gtr) = 1, we obtain
pK(Gtr) ≥
1
1 + ρK
,
pK(Π \ Gtr) ≤
ρK
1 + ρK
,
which is (4).
B.2. Proof of Lemma 1

1

promotable set, Assumption 4 gives
pK
␀Gprom

(ε)

.
According to the Assumption 3, we have TV(Qt,j,pK) = supA⊆Π |Qt,j(A) −pK(A)|, which implies
that for every subset A ⊆Π,
Qt,j(A) ≥pK(A) −τt.
Taking A = Gprom
t,j
(ε) yields
Qt,j
␀Gprom
t,j
(ε)

−τt.
Because probabilities are nonnegative, then we obtain
Qt,j

(ε)

For the near-oracle safe set, Assumption 5 similarly gives

This proves the lemma.

V dep

t
(π | ˜π)
 ≤ξt.
Proof. By definition,
V dep
t
(π | ˜π) −V rep
t
(π | ˜π) = EP dep
t
[zt,π,˜π] −EP rep
t
[zt,π,˜π],
and since zt,π,˜π ∈Ft, the absolute value is at most
sup
f∈Ft
EP dep
t
f −EP rep
t
f
 = ξt.
Lemma B.2 (Adaptive uniform confidence event). Under Assumption 2, we have
P(Ct) ≥1 −δt, where Ct :=
\
(π,˜π)∈Et
{|bµt(π | ˜π) −V rep
t
(π | ˜π)| ≤radt(π | ˜π)}
Proof. Fix a period t. The AlphaInventory procedure generates at most Nt confidence statements in
period t: |At,0| −1 pre-loop comparisons against πref
t
and at most two additional queried pairs per
inner-loop round. For any one such evaluated pair (π, ˜π), condition on the query-time sigma-field
Gt(π, ˜π). By Assumption 2, the replay observations are conditionally independent, bounded in
[−Bt,Bt], and have conditional mean V rep
t
(π | ˜π). Hoeffding’s inequality yields
P(|bµt(π | ˜π) −V rep
t
(π | ˜π)| > radt(π | ˜π) | Gt(π, ˜π)) ≤δt
Nt
.
where the radius is defined as radt(π | ˜π) = Bt
q
2 log(2Nt/δt)
mt
. Taking expectations preserves the same
upper bound unconditionally. A union bound over the at most Nt realized confidence statements
therefore gives
P(Cc
t ) ≤δt.
Hence P(Ct) ≥1 −δt.
B.4. Proof of Lemma 2
Recall that Gprom
t,j
(ε) ≜

π ∈Π : g(π) = 1,∆safe
t
(π) ≥0,∆imp
t,j (π) ≥0
	
, then for any π ∈Gprom
t,j
(ε). By
definition, we expand the ∆safe
t
(π) ≥0,∆imp
t,j (π) ≥0 and obtain:
V rep
t
(π | πref
t ) ≥ξt + 2 radt(π | πref
t ),
(B. 3)
V rep
t
(π | πch
t,j−1) ≥ε + ξt + 2 radt(π | πch
t,j−1).
(B. 4)
We now prove the claim in two steps.

Huang et al : AlphaInventory: Evolving White-Box Inventory Policies with Deployment Guarantees
6
Step 1: On Ct, every promotable candidate passes the replay gate. Assume throughout this step
that Ct holds and that π ∈Gprom
t,j
(ε). Because Ct holds, the replay estimate against the reference
baseline satisfies
bµt(π | πref
t ) −V rep
t
(π | πref
t )
 ≤radt(π | πref

(π | πref

Subtracting another radius from both sides yields
LCBt(π | πref
t ) = bµt(π | πref
t ) −radt(π | πref
t ) ≥V rep
t
(π | πref
t ) −2 radt(π | πref
t ).
Now apply (B. 3), which implies V rep
t
(π | πref
t ) −2 radt(π | πref
t ) ≥ξt, combining the last two displays
gives LCBt(π | πref
t ) ≥ξt. Thus the safety gate is passed.
Exactly the same reasoning applies to the comparison with the current champion. Since Ct holds,
we can also obtain
bµt(π | πch
t,j−1) ≥V rep
t
(π | πch
t,j−1) −radt(π | πch
t,j−1).
Subtracting one more radius gives
LCBt(π | πch
t,j−1) = bµt(π | πch
t,j−1) −radt(π | πch
t,j−1) ≥V rep
t
(π | πch
t,j−1) −2 radt(π | πch
t,j−1).
Using (B. 4), we obtain
V rep
t
(π | πch
t,j−1) −2 radt(π | πch
t,j−1) ≥ε + ξt,
and therefore LCBt(π | πch
t,j−1) ≥ε + ξt. Thus the improvement gate is also passed. Since both gate
inequalities hold, the proposal-gate rule accepts and promotes π.
Step 2: A promoted promotable candidate is safe and genuinely improving under deployment. We
now transfer the replay guarantees to deployment. By Lemma B.1, for every relevant pair (π, eπ), we
have
V dep
t
(π | eπ) −V rep
t
(π | eπ)
 ≤ξt. Equivalently,
V dep
t
(π | eπ) ≥V rep
t
(π | eπ) −ξt.
Apply this first with eπ = πref
t . Then we have V dep
t
(π | πref
t ) ≥V rep
t
(π | πref
t ) −ξt.. Using (B. 3), we
obtain
V dep
t
(π | πref
t ) ≥
␀ξt + 2 radt(π | πref
t )

−ξt = 2 radt(π | πref
t ) ≥0.
Hence π is deployment-safe relative to the reference baseline: V dep
t
(π | πref
t ) ≥0.
Next apply the same transfer inequality with eπ = πch
t,j−1. Then
V dep
t
(π | πch
t,j−1) ≥V rep
t
(π | πch
t,j−1) −ξt.
Invoking (B. 4), we get
V dep
t
(π | πch
t,j−1) ≥
␀ε + ξt + 2 radt(π | πch
t,j−1)

−ξt = ε + 2 radt(π | πch
t,j−1) ≥ε.


--- Page 42 ---
Huang et al : AlphaInventory: Evolving White-Box Inventory Policies with Deployment Guarantees
7
Therefore the promoted candidate is not only replay-certified but also truly better under deployment:
V dep
t
(π | πch
t,j−1) ≥ε.
Combining Steps 1 and 2 proves the claim: if Ct holds and π ∈Gprom
t,j
(ε), then the proposal-gate
rule promotes π, and the promoted policy satisfies
V dep
t
(π | πref
t ) ≥0,
V dep
t
(π | πch
t,j−1) ≥ε.
B.5. Proof of Theorem 2
Condition throughout on the event Etr. The proof relies on Lemma 1 and Lemma 2. Since the initial
comparator πch
t,0 may already be the strongest safety certified baseline or incumbent fallback policy,
successful promotion is benchmarked against this stronger period-initial fallback. Define
F prom
t,0
:= Ω,
F prom
t,j
:=
j\
r=1
{πt,r /∈Gprom
t,r
(ε)},
j = 1,...,J.
The event F prom
t,j
means that, over the first j rounds, none of the generated candidates belongs to
the corresponding promotable set. Equivalently,
F prom
t,J
=
n
∀j ≤J : πt,j /∈Gprom
t,j
(ε)
o
= Sc
t , where St := {∃j ≤J : πt,j ∈Gprom
t,j
(ε)}.
By Lemma 1, for each round j,
P
␀πt,j ∈Gprom
t,j
(ε) | Ft,j−1

= Qt,j
␀Gprom
t,j
(ε)

≥qt.
Therefore,
P(F prom
t,j
) = E
h
1F prom
t,j−1P
␀πt,j /∈Gprom
t,j
(ε) | Ft,j−1
i
≤(1 −qt)P(F prom
t,j−1).
Here, F prom
t,j−1 is Ft,j−1-measurable. Moreover, since the conditional success probability is bounded
below by qt, the corresponding conditional failure probability is bounded above by 1 −qt.
Applying this inequality recursively gives
P(Sc
t ) = P(F prom
t,J
) ≤(1 −qt)J.
Next, Lemma B.2 implies that
P(Ct) ≥1 −δt.
On the event Ct ∩St, Lemma 2 guarantees the existence of a promoted candidate that satisfies
deployment safety and achieves ε-improvement. Hence, Ct ∩St ⊆Eprom
t
(ε). It follows that
P
␀Eprom
t
(ε)

≥1 −δt −(1 −qt)J,
which proves Theorem 2.


--- Page 43 ---
Huang et al : AlphaInventory: Evolving White-Box Inventory Policies with Deployment Guarantees
8
B.6. Proof of Theorem 3
Condition on Etr. We first control the discovery event Dt. Define
Dt := {∃j ≤J : πt,j ∈Πsafe,νt
t
},
F safe
t,0 := Ω,
Dt can be interpreted as the event that, within the J proposal rounds of period t, the search process
hits at least one candidate in the near-oracle safe class Πsafe,νt
t
. For j = 1,...,J,
F safe
t,j :=
j\
r=1
{πt,r /∈Πsafe,νt
t
}.
Then F safe
t,J = Dc
t.
Recall that Ct is the period-t confidence event under which all replay estimates used by the
algorithm are simultaneously accurate. Thus, Ct guarantees that replay-based certificates are reliable,
and Dt guarantees that the active pool contains a candidate that is close to the best policy in the
certifiably safe class.
Accordingly, the proof proceeds in three steps:
1. First, we bound the probability of Dt, which corresponds to the event that the near-oracle safe
class is hit at least once within period t.
2. Second, on the event Ct, we establish deployment safety.
3. Third, on the event Ct ∩Dt, we derive the oracle-safe deployment gap bound.
Step 1: Control Probability of Dt.
By Lemma 1, for each round j, P(πt,j ∈Πsafe,νt
t
| Ft,j−1) = Qt,j
␀Πsafe,νt
t

≥¯qt. Hence
P(F safe
t,j ) = E
h
1F safe
t,j−1P(πt,j /∈Πsafe,νt
t
| Ft,j−1)
i
≤(1 −¯qt)P(F safe
t,j−1),
so by induction,
P(Dc
t) = P(F safe
t,J ) ≤(1 −¯qt)J.
Step 2: Safety on Ct. If Afeas
t
= ∅, then by definition dt = πref
t , and therefore V dep
t
(dt | πref
t ) = 0.
Suppose now that Afeas
t
̸= ∅. Then dt ∈Afeas
t
, based on definition of Afeas
t
, we have:
LCBt(dt | πref
t ) ≥ξt.
Since Ct holds, by definition we have |bµt (dt | πref
t
) −V rep
t
(dt | πref
t
)| ≤radt (dt | πref
t
), which implies
V rep
t
␀dt | πref
t

≥bµt
␀dt | πref
t

−radt
␀dt | πref
t

= LCBt
␀dt | πref
t

≥ξt.
By Lemma B.1, we have V dep
t
(dt | πref
t ) ≥0. The deployment rule itself is safety screened. It does
not select arbitrarily from the entire active pool. Instead, it only selects from the set of candidates


--- Page 44 ---
Huang et al : AlphaInventory: Evolving White-Box Inventory Policies with Deployment Guarantees
9
satisfying LCB ≥ξt. Here, ξt is chosen to match the worst-case replay-to-deployment mismatch
budget. Therefore, on the event Ct, any candidate that appears safe in replay is also guaranteed to
be no worse than the baseline after deployment.
Step 3: Oracle-safe deployment-gap bound on Ct ∩Dt. Assume now that Ct ∩Dt holds. Then
there exists some round j∗≤J such that πt,j∗∈Πsafe,νt
t
.
Because every structurally valid generated candidate is inserted into the active pool and Πsafe,νt
t
⊆
{πref
t } ∪{π : g(π) = 1}, we may choose ˜πt ∈Πsafe,νt
t
∩At. If ˜πt = πref
t , then
V safe,∗
t
−V dep
t
(dt | πref
t ) ≤V safe,∗
t
−V dep
t
(πref
t
| πref
t ) ≤νt ≤Γt(˜πt,dt),
where the first inequality uses results in Step 2, which states that dt is not worse that πref
t , and the
second uses ˜πt ∈Πsafe,νt
t
. Therefore, it remains to consider the case ˜πt ̸= πref
t .
Because ˜πt ∈Πsafe,νt
t
\ {πref
t } ⊆Πsafe
t
, we have ∆safe
t
(˜πt) ≥0, that is,
V rep
t
(˜πt | πref
t ) ≥ξt + 2 radt(˜πt | πref
t ).
On Ct, this implies
LCBt(˜πt | πref
t ) ≥V rep
t
(˜πt | πref
t ) −2 radt(˜πt | πref
t ) ≥ξt,
so ˜πt ∈Afeas
t
. By the deployment rule, dt maximizes UCBt(· | πref
t ) over Afeas
t
, so
bµt(dt | πref
t ) + radt(dt | πref
t ) ≥bµt(˜πt | πref
t ) + radt(˜πt | πref
t ).
Since Ct holds,
V rep
t
(˜πt | πref
t ) −V rep
t
(dt | πref
t ) ≤2 radt(˜πt | πref
t ) + 2 radt(dt | πref
t ).
By Lemma B.1,
V dep
t
(˜πt | πref
t ) −V dep
t
(dt | πref
t ) ≤2 radt(˜πt | πref
t ) + 2 radt(dt | πref
t ) + 2ξt.
By definition of Πsafe,νt

, we have V safe,∗

−V dep
t
(˜πt | πref
t ) ≤νt. Hence
V safe,∗

−V dep

(dt | πref

(B. 5)
Finally, define GT := TT
t=1(Ct ∩Dt). Previous result show that P(Dc

P(GT) ≥1 −

δt + (1 −¯qt)J
.
On GT, Step 2 gives safety for every period, and Step 3 gives the per-period oracle-safe deployment-gap
bound. Summing (B. 5) over t = 1,...,T yields
T

≤
T

Γt(˜πt,dt),
which proves Theorem 3.

Appendix D: Synthetic Data Construction Details
This appendix records the additional technical details of the synthetic generator beyond the sum-
mary in Section 4.1. The synthetic data used in this study, together with the inference traces of
AlphaInventory, will be released.
D.1. Generation Primitives and Covariate Construction
We construct 47 synthetic seed datasets, each spanning daily observations from January 1,
2024 to December 31, 2025. The datasets cover multiple inventory-relevant environments, including
consumer products, industrial spare parts, restaurant ingredients, medical supplies, power-grid load,
cloud-computing demand, EV battery supply chains, and construction materials.
Let r = 1,...,R index calendar days, where R = 731. For each seed dataset j and day r, we
generate an observed covariate vector xj,r, an optional textual note nj,r, a latent regime variable zj,r,
an event-state variable ej,r, and realized demand yj,r.
The observed covariates are derived from a shared latent environment and then instantiated in a
dataset-specific manner. The shared primitives include:


--- Page 46 ---
Huang et al : AlphaInventory: Evolving White-Box Inventory Policies with Deployment Guarantees
11
• calendar signals, such as month, day-of-week, weekend indicators, and selected derived flags;
• weather signals, generated from seasonal components, autoregressive disturbances, and
occasional extreme events;
• promotion and festival signals, formed by event-centered promotional windows with decaying
intensity and superimposed short-lived shocks;
• macro or cost indices, generated as stochastic trends with occasional spikes or drawdowns.
For example, temperature is generated as
Tempr = α0 + α1 sin
2π(dr −ϕ)
365.25

+ ur + εw
r ,
where dr is the day-of-year, ur is an AR(1) disturbance, and εw
r captures rare weather shocks. Other
weather variables, such as humidity, precipitation, and UV index, are then generated conditionally
on season and temperature.
The final covariate schema is not shared across datasets. Instead, each seed dataset selects and
transforms a domain-relevant subset of the shared primitives and augments them with domain-
specific proxies. Representative examples include weather and promotion variables for retail-like
products, utilization and maintenance proxies for industrial spare parts, admission and influenza
signals for medical supplies, and traffic or latency signals for digital services. To avoid a trivial
mapping from domain to schema, we apply feature-subset randomization: after a candidate feature
pool is constructed, each dataset retains only a random subset of features, while always preserving
identifiers, notes, and the target demand variable.
D.2. Demand Law, Hidden States, and Nonstationarity
For each dataset j and day r, demand is drawn from a time-varying distribution
yj,r ∼Dj,r
␀θj,r

,
where both the family Dj,r and parameter vector θj,r may vary across datasets and over time. The
generator allows several demand families, including:
• negative binomial distributions for over-dispersed count demand;
• zero-inflated or hurdle-like models for intermittent demand;
• mixture models for regime-switching demand;
• continuous positive demand for abstract entities such as regional power load.
In the count-demand setting, the conditional mean is specified as
log µj,r = βj,0(r) + βj(r)⊤xj,r + γ⊤
j zj,r + δ⊤
j ej,r,


--- Page 47 ---
Huang et al : AlphaInventory: Evolving White-Box Inventory Policies with Deployment Guarantees
12
where βj,0(r) is a time-varying intercept, βj(r) is a potentially time-varying coefficient vector, zj,r
denotes latent demand regimes, and ej,r denotes the event-state process. Demand may then be
sampled, for example, from
yj,r ∼NB
␀µj,r,κj,r

,
with dispersion parameter κj,r allowed to vary with latent volatility. In intermittent-demand settings,
we additionally introduce a zero-generation mechanism,
yj,r =
(
0,
with probability p(0)
j,r,
˜yj,r,
otherwise,
where ˜yj,r follows the corresponding base demand law.
Many datasets include latent mixture regimes. Specifically, for some seeds,
yj,r ∼
Kj
X
k=1
π(k)
j,r D(k)
j,r ,
where the mixture weights π(k)
j,r are time-varying and may depend on observed covariates and latent
conditions. This mechanism allows bursty, clustered, or intermittent behavior even under similar
observed contexts.
The synthetic suite incorporates several forms of nonstationarity:
1. baseline drift, through gradual variation in βj,0(r);
2. coefficient drift, through time variation in βj(r);
3. structural breaks, representing abrupt changes in demand formation;
4. mixture-weight drift, through time-varying regime probabilities;
5. covariate shift, through evolution of the latent environment that generates xj,r.
Accordingly, the suite exhibits both temporal variation in the covariate distribution and temporal
variation in the conditional law of demand.
D.3. Disruptive Events, Note Persistence, and Within-Archetype Heterogeneity
To incorporate unstructured information, we generate rare disruptive events such as typhoons, recalls,
strikes, outages, pandemic waves, and food-safety rumors. Each event begins at some date τ and
lasts for Λevt days. A textual note is recorded only at event onset:
nj,r =
(
event description,
r = τ and the event is observed,
∅,
otherwise.
Thus, the note process records the onset of an observed disruption, while persistence is represented
through the latent event-state process rather than repeated annotations.


--- Page 48 ---
Huang et al : AlphaInventory: Evolving White-Box Inventory Policies with Deployment Guarantees
13
Although the note appears only on the first day, the event continues to affect demand through a
decaying latent impact:
e(ℓ)
j,r =
(
κj,ℓωℓ(r −τ),
τ ≤r < τ + Λevt,
0,
otherwise,
where ℓindexes the event type, κj,ℓis a dataset-specific intensity, and ωℓ(·) is a decreasing decay
profile. In implementation, both the conditional mean and the variance may respond to e(ℓ)
j,r. The sign
and magnitude of event effects are domain-dependent; the same event label may increase demand in
one domain and decrease it in another.
To avoid excessive similarity within a broad archetype, we introduce heterogeneity at three levels.
First, variant-level heterogeneity assigns different seeds within the same archetype to different
demand-generation variants, thereby changing the functional form of the generator rather than
only its coefficients. Second, feature-level heterogeneity arises from feature-subset randomization, so
datasets within the same archetype need not share the same observable schema. Third, shock-level
heterogeneity randomizes the timing, duration, observability, and intensity of disruptive events at
the seed level. As a result, the final suite combines shared generation primitives with dataset-specific
randomization while preserving a common construction template across seeds.
Appendix E: Estimating the Replay–Deployment Discrepancy Budget ξt in Real Data
In the main text, the replay–deployment discrepancy budget ξt is defined through the integral
probability metric
ξt ≡DFt(P dep
t
,P rep
t
),
and enters both the promotion gate and the final deployment rule as a period-level safety margin. In
implementation, however, the deployment distribution P dep
t
is not directly observable at decision
time. As a result, ξt must be estimated from historical replay-to-forward discrepancies rather than
treated as a known quantity. This appendix presents a practical estimation strategy for ξt in real
data. We first introduce a general methodology that is independent of any specific dataset, and then
instantiate it for the Dunnhumby Complete Journey benchmark used in Section 5.2.
E.1. General estimation principle
Fix a period t. Let Rt denote the replay window available at decision time, and let Dt denote the
corresponding forward window used only for retrospective validation.
Retrospective target. For any evaluated policy–comparator pair (π, ˜π), define the empirical replay
gain
bV rep
t
(π | ˜π) =
1
|Rt|
X
ω∈Rt
␀Ct(˜π;ω) −Ct(π;ω)

,


--- Page 49 ---
Huang et al : AlphaInventory: Evolving White-Box Inventory Policies with Deployment Guarantees
14
and the empirical forward gain
bV fwd
t
(π | ˜π) =
1
|Dt|
X
ω∈Dt
␀Ct(˜π;ω) −Ct(π;ω)

.
The realized replay-to-forward discrepancy for that pair is then
∆t(π, ˜π) :=
bV fwd
t
(π | ˜π) −bV rep
t
(π | ˜π)
.
Let Et denote the set of policy–comparator pairs evaluated in period t. A natural ex post benchmark
is the oracle discrepancy
ξoracle
t
:= max
(π,˜π)∈Et ∆t(π, ˜π),
or, more robustly, its upper empirical quantile
ξoracle
t
(β) := Q1−β

{∆t(π, ˜π) : (π, ˜π) ∈Et}

,
β ∈(0,1).
The quantity ξoracle
t
is useful for retrospective diagnosis, but it cannot be used directly online because
Dt lies in the future relative to the decision at period t. When Et includes the realized candidate
policies generated during search, ξoracle
t
should be interpreted as an ex post operational calibration
target attached to the realized evaluation set rather than as a method-free environment parameter.
If a method-independent target is desired, the same construction can instead be applied to a fixed
archived library of policy–comparator pairs.
Online budget. For implementation, we therefore distinguish between:
1. the retrospective target ξoracle
t
, used only ex post for evaluation and calibration; and
2. the online budget bξt, used in the gate and deployment rule.
The former is an ex post diagnostic target; the latter is the only quantity used online.
To construct bξt, we separate two roles for the shift features. First, retrospective calibration features
uret
t
= ϕ(Rt,Dt) may use realized forward outcomes ex post to learn the mapping from realized shift
patterns to oracle discrepancies. Second, online proxy features uon
t = ϕ(Rt, eDt) use only pre-decision
covariates or deployment proxies, where eDt denotes a proxy for the current deployment regime.
Typical components of these feature vectors include differences in demand moments, changes in zero-
demand ratio, covariate-distribution shifts, seasonality indicators, and differences in baseline-policy
costs.
Using historical periods s < t, we form calibration pairs {(uret
s ,ξoracle
s
)}t−1
s=1, or, more robustly,
{(uret
s ,ξoracle
s
(β))}t−1
s=1. All calibration of the quantile model is performed using slices strictly preceding
the evaluation slice, or on a calibration pool disjoint from that slice, so that no forward window from
the reported test workspace is used to fit bξt. We then fit an upper conditional quantile model
bq1−α(u) ≈Q1−α(ξoracle | u),


--- Page 50 ---
Huang et al : AlphaInventory: Evolving White-Box Inventory Policies with Deployment Guarantees
15
and define the operational safety budget by bξt = bq1−α(uon
t ) + bt, where bt ≥0 is an optional finite-
sample inflation term. The resulting quantity bξt can be plugged directly into the certification gate
and deployment rule:
LCBt(π | πref
t
) ≥bξt,
LCBt(π | πch
t,j−1) ≥ε + bξt.
E.2. Operational estimators and recommended use
The construction above can be implemented in several complementary ways.
Historical quantile calibration. A simple conservative estimator is obtained by pooling realized
replay-to-forward discrepancies from the most recent Mhist historical periods:
bξhist
t
= Q1−α

t−1
[
s=t−Mhist
{∆s(π, ˜π) : (π, ˜π) ∈Es}

.
This estimator is easy to compute and does not require fitting a predictive model, but it does not
adapt to the current period’s specific shift pattern.
Shift-conditioned quantile estimation. A more adaptive estimator uses the online proxy feature
vector uon
t :
bξshift
t
= bq1−α(uon
t ) + bt.
Possible choices for bq1−α include linear quantile regression, tree-based quantile regression, and
conformalized quantile regression. This approach is particularly useful when replay–deployment
mismatch varies systematically with observable state changes.
IPM plug-in estimator. Because the theoretical definition of ξt is IPM-based, one may also
construct an estimator that directly approximates the discrepancy between replay and deployment
distributions. Let ψ(ω) ∈Rd be a path embedding, and let bP rep,ψ
t
and bP dep,ψ
t
denote empirical replay
and deployment-proxy distributions in the embedding space. Then one may define
bξIP M
t
= bDFt
␀ bP dep,ψ
t
, bP rep,ψ
t

,
for a chosen function class Ft. This estimator is conceptually closest to the main theory, although it
requires the additional design of an embedding map and a deployment proxy.
In practice, we recommend a conservative combination:
bξt = max{bξhist
t
, bξshift
t
}.
In the CJ instantiation below, we use this combined form to define the operational discrepancy
budget. A concrete implementation should additionally report the numerical choices of Mhist, α, bt,
and the conditional-quantile model class used to construct bq1−α.


--- Page 51 ---
Huang et al : AlphaInventory: Evolving White-Box Inventory Policies with Deployment Guarantees
16
Interpretation. From an operational viewpoint, bξt should be interpreted as a conservative safety
margin rather than a fully identified structural parameter. Its purpose is to upper-bound replay-to-
forward gain errors with sufficiently high probability, thereby preserving the deployment guarantees
encoded in the gate.
E.3. Instantiation for the Dunnhumby Complete Journey dataset
We now instantiate the above methodology for the Dunnhumby Complete Journey (CJ) benchmark
used in Section 5.2. In the main experiment, each workspace is constructed from a temporal slice
consisting of 365 historical days followed by 30 evaluation days. Each slice also contains six exogenous
features:
is_weekend, is_holiday, discount_rate, is_on_display, is_in_mailer, day_of_week.
This structure makes the CJ data particularly suitable for replay-to-forward discrepancy calibration.
For each temporal slice t, we define:
• the first 365 days as the replay window Rt;
• the subsequent 30 days as the forward validation window Dt.
For every evaluated pair (π, ˜π) ∈Et, including pre-loop baseline/incumbent versus reference pairs
and inner-loop generated-candidate pairs, we compute
∆t(π, ˜π) =
bV fwd
t
(π | ˜π) −bV rep
t
(π | ˜π)
.
This yields the ex post oracle discrepancy ξoracle
t
= max(π,˜π)∈Et ∆t(π, ˜π), or its quantile analogue. In
this form, the CJ oracle discrepancy is defined relative to the realized evaluation set in the slice. If a
method-independent calibration target is preferred, the same construction can instead be applied to
a fixed archived policy library.
For retrospective calibration, we define a CJ-specific feature vector
uret
t
=

| ¯dDt −¯dRt|,|σDt −σRt|,|ρ0
Dt −ρ0
Rt|,
|discountDt −discountRt|,|displayDt −displayRt|,
|mailerDt −mailerRt|,|holidayDt −holidayRt|

.
where ¯d denotes mean demand, σ denotes demand standard deviation, and ρ0 denotes the zero-
demand ratio. This retrospective form uses realized forward-window statistics only for ex post
calibration. In online deployment, it should be replaced by an observable proxy vector uon
t
computed
from pre-decision covariates or other deployment-time signals.


--- Page 52 ---
Huang et al : AlphaInventory: Evolving White-Box Inventory Policies with Deployment Guarantees
17
Using historical slices strictly preceding the evaluation slice, or a calibration pool disjoint from that
slice, we fit an upper-quantile model bq1−α(u) ≈Q1−α(ξoracle | u). In particular, no forward window
from the reported test workspace is used to fit the operational budget. We then define
bξshift,CJ
t
= bq1−α(uon
t ) + bt,
bξhist,CJ
t
= Q1−α

t−1
[
s=t−Mhist
{∆s(π, ˜π) : (π, ˜π) ∈Es}

,
and set the CJ operational discrepancy budget to
bξCJ
t
= max{bξhist,CJ
t
, bξshift,CJ
t
}.
Finally, we replace ξt in the proposal-gate and deployment rule by bξCJ
t
:
LCBt(π | πref
t
) ≥bξCJ
t
,
LCBt(π | πch
t,j−1) ≥ε + bξCJ
t
.
This procedure preserves the distinction between the retrospective target ξoracle
t
and the online
budget bξCJ
t
. The former is used only ex post for calibration and diagnosis; the latter is the only
quantity used in the gate and deployment rule.
Appendix F: Practical Small-Sample Blockwise t-Based Replay Radius Adjustment for Replay
Certification
The main text adopts the Hoeffding-style confidence radius in (7) as a conservative, distribution-free
replay certificate. In practice, however, short replay windows may lead to substantial finite-sample
conservatism. This appendix is mainly motivated by shorter replay windows, especially the 100+30
synthetic setting of Section 5.1, although the same idea can be used more broadly as a practical
adjustment. The purpose of this appendix is therefore purely operational: we introduce a small-
sample-friendly blockwise t-based replay radius for practical screening. This adjustment is intended as
a practical screening heuristic rather than a finite-sample-valid replacement for the formal Hoeffding
certificate in the main theory.
A practical complication in inventory replay is that daily gain observations may exhibit short-range
dependence due to inventory carryover, replenishment delay, and lead-time effects. To reduce the
impact of such local dependence, we first aggregate daily replay gains into non-overlapping blocks.
For a candidate policy π and comparator ˜π, recall the daily replay gain
Zt,ℓ(π | ˜π) = Ct(˜π;ωt,ℓ) −Ct(π;ωt,ℓ),
ℓ= 1,...,mt.
Let the replay window be partitioned into Kt non-overlapping blocks Bt,1,...,Bt,Kt, each of length
bt, except possibly the last block. We define the blockwise average gain by
Gt,k(π | ˜π) =
1
|Bt,k|
X
ℓ∈Bt,k
Zt,ℓ(π | ˜π),
k = 1,...,Kt.


--- Page 53 ---
Huang et al : AlphaInventory: Evolving White-Box Inventory Policies with Deployment Guarantees
18
The corresponding blockwise mean and sample variance are
¯Gt(π | ˜π) = 1
Kt
Kt
X
k=1
Gt,k(π | ˜π),
s2
G,t(π | ˜π) =
1
Kt −1
Kt
X
k=1
␀Gt,k(π | ˜π) −¯Gt(π | ˜π)
2 .
We then define the practical blockwise t-based replay radius by
d
rad
prac
t
(π | ˜π) = t1−αt(π,˜π), Kt−1 · sG,t(π | ˜π)
√Kt
,
where t1−α,ν denotes the (1 −α)-quantile of the Student-t distribution with ν degrees of freedom.
The resulting practical lower and upper confidence bounds are
LCBprac
t
(π | ˜π) = ¯Gt(π | ˜π) −d
rad
prac
t
(π | ˜π),
UCBprac
t
(π | ˜π) = ¯Gt(π | ˜π) + d
rad
prac
t
(π | ˜π).
For multiple testing across the evaluated-pair budget Nt, a simple conservative choice is αt(π, ˜π) =
δt
2Nt . This mirrors the role of Nt in the formal Hoeffding construction while remaining more sample-
adaptive in practice.
Recommended block size and use in short replay windows. In inventory applications, we recommend
bt = max{7, Lt + 1},
where Lt is the effective lead time in the current workspace. When lead time is fixed or not explicitly
modeled in the replay implementation, a default choice bt = 7 is a reasonable weekly aggregation
rule. In a 100-day replay window, this yields approximately Kt ≈14 effective block observations.
Thus, in the synthetic 100+30 setting, the natural default is weekly blocking with bt = 7. For longer
replay windows such as the CJ 365+30 slices, the same adjustment can still be used as an optional
practical screen, but the small-sample motivation is weaker.
